PAKISTAN
R
UPEE ANCHORED, GADANI PAUSES
Pakistan closes the month as the basin's currency anchor. USD/PKR held at approximately 278.58
Ru pee anchored. on May 28, essentially unchanged across three consecutive weeks and now stronger by roughly
1.6% year-to-date, the only sub-continent currency to have appreciated against the dollar since the
war opened. The configuration that began as a Week 19 vulnerability, when the 10.9% April CPI
shock forced the State Bank into its first rate hike in three years, has fully matured into a Week 22
structural advantage. The 100 basis point move to 11.5%, sustained remittance inflows through the
Eid period, and disciplined reserve management have held the rupee steady through every macro
shock the quarter has produced.
Eid al-Adha, observed May 27 to 29 in Pakistan, has brought Gadani to its seasonal halt. As at
Eid pauses. Chattogram and Alang, no new beaching activity is expected through the holiday, and the
resumption of the working week will coincide with the practical closure of the pre-monsoon
window. The tentative US-Iran deal, were it to hold, would over a multi-month horizon erode the
Gulf proximity premium that has defined Gadani's structural position through the conflict, by
restoring the routing economics that the blockade suspended. That erosion is a Q3 and Q4 question.
It is not a question the holiday week will answer.
Local steel plate prices at PKR 195,000 per ton held the Week 20 step-up through the holiday, with
Position intact. the USD equivalent near USD 700 per ton on the steady rupee. Gadani's absolute pricing remains
the firmest of the year, and the gap to India sits near USD 280 per LDT. Three HKC-certified yards

Position intact. the USD equivalent near USD 700 per ton on the steady rupee. Gadani's absolute pricing remains
the firmest of the year, and the gap to India sits near USD 280 per LDT. Three HKC-certified yards
remain operational with further certifications in the pipeline, and the candidate pool aligned with
Gadani's capacity has not contracted. Pakistan ends the quarter with the strongest structural
position in the basin, the most stable currency, and the firmest pricing, and like every other
destination, with no executed tonnage to show for any of it. The position is intact. The ships are
not arriving. Both statements have been true all quarter.

TURKEY
L
IRA BREAKS, FORECAST SLIPS
T urkey enters Week 22 with USD/TRY at approximately 45.90, a fresh record low surpassing the
W eek 21 reference of 45.58 and touching an intraday extreme near 46.09. The lira has weakened
Fresh record.
a pproximately 1.75% over the past month and 17.4% over the past 12 months. Alone among the
basin's currencies, the lira made a new low in the week of the peace deal, a divergence that reflects
the fact that Turkey's pressures are domestic and structural rather than Hormuz-driven. The Central
Bank of Turkey's controlled-slide framework continues to function: the rate of nominal depreciation
is being managed, even as the level it is converging toward continues to deteriorate.
The TCMB has now revised its end-2026 inflation projection to 26%, materially above the 15% to 21%
F orecast slips.
range it set in February, an explicit acknowledgement that the disinflation strategy has been
overtaken by the energy and food price shocks transmitted through the conflict. The April CPI at
32.37%, with monthly inflation at 4.18%, remains the operative read, and independent estimates from
ENAG and the Istanbul Chamber continue to track
snippet: That erosion is a Q3 and Q4 question. It is not a question the holiday week will answer. Local steel plate prices at PKR 195,000 per ton held the Week 20 step-up through the holiday, with Position int

text:
MB has now revised its end-2026 inflation projection to 26%, materially above the 15% to 21%
F orecast slips.
range it set in February, an explicit acknowledgement that the disinflation strategy has been
overtaken by the energy and food price shocks transmitted through the conflict. The April CPI at
32.37%, with monthly inflation at 4.18%, remains the operative read, and independent estimates from
ENAG and the Istanbul Chamber continue to track materially higher. The deal-driven Brent decline
to USD 96 may, if sustained, offer the first genuine disinflation tailwind Turkey has seen all year. "If
sustained" is carrying a great deal of weight in that sentence.
At USD 268 to USD 290 per LDT across vessel types, Aliaga remains structurally uncompetitive for
N iche persists.
mainstream tonnage. The lira's break to 45.90 has firmed Turkish offers marginally in USD terms,
but the gap to Pakistan at USD 445 to USD 480 and Bangladesh at USD 460 to USD 495 remains
unbridgeable. The gap to India at USD 415 to USD 420 narrowed slightly as the rupee recovered.
Turkey's relevance this Q2 remains confined to the EU-regulated tonnage segment, where Basel
Convention compliance overrides price. The tentative peace deal does not change this. Aliaga has
operated in a different market all quarter, and will continue to after the strait reopens.

MARKET SUMMARY
This week, the shipping industry and broader maritime market attention remained
focused on Posidonia in Greece, a key platform for industry engagement, market
insights, and commercial discussions. Relevant developments and key takeaways from
the event will be consolidated and shared in the upcoming weekly update.
INDIA
The Indian market remained highly unpredictable this week, with prices holding
steady and overall sentiment staying soft amid limited activity.
Buyer appetite remains firm nonetheless, supported by a steady flow of vessels
arriving in India, including a notable share of dark fleet tonnage, while improved steel
availability has traders looking to tighten recycling payment terms.
End prices remain the same whether recycling a standard vessel or a dark fleet unit;
however, with dark fleet tonnage acquired at cheaper rates, conventional recyclers are
finding it increasingly hard to compete.
On the policy front, the Government issued its first-ever ship recycling credit note
under the new scheme, confirming the mechanism is now operational, a positive step
towards strengthening India's competitiveness as a recycling destination over the
longer term.
BANGLADESH
The Bangladesh market remained largely the same as last week, with conditions
broadly unchanged.
Following the Eid break, yards have resumed operations, though activity stays quiet for
now, with no sales reported and overall buying reduced.
Rates have softened alongside the slower pace of business, keeping the market
subdued at present.
That said, momentum is expected to build over the course of this week as operations
settle back into rhythm.

[Page 3]
PAKISTAN
With the market closed for the Eid holidays over the past week, no sales were
concluded

[Page 3]
PAKISTAN
With the market closed for the Eid holidays over the past week, no sales were
concluded and conditions held steady from before the break.
Buyers remain keen for fresh tonnage, though limited vessel availability continues to
constrain purchasing.
On the regulatory front, Pakistan passed new ship recycling legislation this week
advancing the Hong Kong Convention, introducing mandatory hazardous-material
surveys and certification for vessels arriving for recycling - a positive step for the
longer-term growth of the country's recycling sector.
TURKIYE
The Turkish market has remained relatively quiet over the past week, with limited
activity, few notable transactions, and cautious sentiment keeping trading levels
subdued.
Despite the current lull, visits and inspections at Turkish yards by several major
container operators offer some encouragement, raising hopes of larger recycling
projects and a pick-up in activity going forward.
Forecasting remains difficult, however, as internal politics, regional tensions, and the
continued depreciation of the Lira sustain a high level of uncertainty over future prices
and market direction.

[Page 2]
MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
The Indian market recorded a modest correction this week; however, this should not
be viewed as a sign of stronger sentiment but rather a market adjustment following
an earlier high-priced vessel sale.
A key development this week was the recyclers' association directing yards to
introduce loading charges, while also asking recyclers to restore stricter payment
discipline, with mills expected to settle on a next-day basis or within a maximum credit
period of 10 days, after payment cycles had loosened during recent geopolitical
tensions.
While these measures are expected to improve liquidity for ship recyclers, the
additional loading costs and tighter payment structure could place pressure on
downstream buyers and mills.
As a result, the local market may face some short-term weakness in the coming week
as participants adjust to the revised commercial framework and assess its impact on
margins.
BANGLADESH
The Bangladesh market softened slightly this week, with local prices easing on a day-
to-day basis.
Despite the softer local market, interest remains visible for mid-sized assets, which
continue to be the preferred tonnage for buyers.
Bangladesh remains the highest-paying recycling destination compared to the other
major markets, supporting continued buyer interest.
PAKISTAN
The Pakistan market remained firm this week, with buyers actively looking for vessels
and overall sentiment staying strong.
Local market conditions continue to support prices, particularly due to a shortage of
available goods.
This shortage has added strength to buyer sentiment and kept the market relatively
positive compared to other destinations.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE

text:
The war premium has finally cracked. Brent crude fell more than 4% on Thursday toward USD 89 per barrel, its lowest level since March,with WTI near USD 86, after President Trump suspended military strikes against Iran that had been scheduled for that evening and told reporters that a deal reopening the Strait of Hormuz could be signed as early as this weekend, likely in Europe. Iran's semi-official Fars news agency reported that Tehran is likely to approve the agreement, though no final text has been confirmed by either side. The Weekly first reported the war premium cracking in Week 15. Nine weeks later, the premium has finally read the report.
The pen is hovering, not yet landed. The week that produced the breakthrough also produced US strikes on Iranian radar and drone sites on June 10 and Iranian missile responses across the Gulf, with mediation now running through Qatar and draft texts still being exchanged. The framework requires Iran to clear all mines from Hormuz within 30 days of signature and bars tolls on the strait, while even a signed deal faces idled production fields, damaged export facilities, and the slow mechanics of restoring confidence to a waterway that has spent a quarter as a war zone. The agreement could be signed this weekend, in Europe, which is at least a new continent for the deadline to miss.
Freight has cooled for the first time in the arc. The Baltic Dry Index eased to 2,818 on June 9 from the June 1 peak of 3,222, with the Baltic Capesize Index at 4,441 and daily Capesize earnings at USD 40,274, down from the USD 49,511 high of the prior week, though Supramax and Handysize both firmed. For the first time since February, the two forces that have kept older tonnage trading rather than beaching, the bunker-cost floor and the freight earnings premium, are softening in the same week. They have chosen the week the beach is closed for the season. The market's timing remains undefeated.
May inflation prints have landed across the basin, and the pattern is uniform: annual rates climbing on accumulated base effects even as the monthly shocks fade. Pakistan's May CPI rose to 11.7%, the highest since June 2024, yet monthly inflation collapsed to 0.5% from 2.5%. Turkey's May CPI edged to 32.61% while its monthly pace fell to 1
snippet: The war premium has finally cracked. Brent crude fell more than 4% on Thursday toward USD 89 per barrel, its lowest level since March,with WTI near USD 86, after President Trump suspended military str

text:
the week the beach is closed for the season. The market's timing remains undefeated.
May inflation prints have landed across the basin, and the pattern is uniform: annual rates climbing on accumulated base effects even as the monthly shocks fade. Pakistan's May CPI rose to 11.7%, the highest since June 2024, yet monthly inflation collapsed to 0.5% from 2.5%. Turkey's May CPI edged to 32.61% while its monthly pace fell to 1.71% from 4.18%. The fire is going out at the stove while the smoke alarm still rings. Currencies extended their repair: the Indian Rupee held its recovery near 95.40, the Pakistani Rupee sustained its 2026 best at approximately 278.39, the Bangladeshi Taka held the floor of its band at 122.75, and the Turkish Lira steadied near its record at approximately 45.85. Detailed country reads follow on pages 2 through 5.
The monsoon now rules the beaching calendar regardless of what is signed in Europe this weekend. But the shape of the next chapter is forming: if the pen lands, the 30-day de-mining clock starts, the strait normalises through July, the bunker floor and freight premium continue to deflate, and the tonnage that nine weeks of war could not release begins to face the question the war deferred. The first chapter of this arc recorded one unbroken fact: the ships did not come. The second chapter opens with the first credible reason to believe they eventually will, arriving, as everything in this market has arrived, precisely when the beach cannot take them. The premium cracks. The pen hovers. The monsoon rules.
For Week 24 of 2026, GMS Market Rankings / Vessel indications are as below.

Beach rests.
operators working down the tonnage delivered through the late-May tide windows. The Letter of
Credit pipeline remains fully permissive, a configuration that now simply waits for the supply side
of the market to produce something to finance. The Bangladesh May CPI release from BBS is the
operative watch item in the coming days; the April print at 9.04% remains the reference, and the
Pakistan and Turkey May reads suggest the annual rate may drift up on base effects even as the
monthly energy shock fades with Brent below USD 90.
The shape of Bangladesh's third quarter is beginning to form around the diplomatic calendar. If
Q3 forms. the framework is signed this weekend and the 30-day de-mining clause runs, the strait normalises
through July, the bunker-cost floor that kept older tonnage trading continues to deflate, and the
freight premium has already begun to ease from its June 1 peak. Chattogram enters that scenario
with the basin's strongest demand position: full plot capacity, cleared financing, the most resilient
currency band in the region, and pricing that holds above Alang and Aliaga at the ton level. The
monsoon governs the next eight weeks. The deal, if it lands, governs everything after. For the first
time since February, the second half looks better than the first.

/INR has held its recovery into a fourth consecutive week at approximately 95.40, consolidating
the retreat from the 96.97 record of May 21. The Reserve Bank of India's June 5 hold at 5.25%, the
R upee steadies.
scrapping of capital gains tax on foreign investment in government bonds, and now the Brent collapse
toward USD 89 have together given the rupee its most supportive configuration since the war opened.
India's import bill, exposed on roughly 70% of crude and 90% of LPG routing through or near Hormuz,
is repricing in real time as the strait's traffic recovers and the war premium deflates. The currency that
spent the war as Asia's worst performer now has, for the first time, a fundamental tailwind rather than
a speculative one.
The relief remains conditional on a signature that has not yet been produced. The RBI's own revised
projections, retail inflation at 5.1% for FY2026-27 and growth trimmed to 6.6%, were set against the Relief conditional.
energy shock as it stood in early June, and a durable Brent settlement below USD 90 would materially
improve both. Foreign institutional investors remained net sellers through early June, and the
framework's unresolved questions, the 30-day de-mining clock, the idled fields, the damaged export
terminals, stand between the weekend's possible signature and the normalised flows the rupee is
beginning to price. The 96.97 record has receded. It has not been repealed.

text:
E SHRUGS
Pakistan's May CPI printed at 11.7% year-on-year, up from 10.9% in April and the highest reading
CP I climbs. since June 2024, with transport at plus 36.8% (versus 29.9%) and housing and utilities elevated at
16.8%. The headline is the war shock's full accumulation in the annual series. The composition tells
the more important story: monthly inflation collapsed to 0.5% from April's 2.5%, the Sensitive Price
Indicator's month-on-month pace eased to 0.7%, and the Wholesale Price Index declined 0.8% on
the month. The annual rate is climbing on base effects while the underlying shock decelerates
sharply. The smoke alarm rings loudest after the fire has begun to go out.
USD/PKR held at approximately 278.39 through the print, sustaining its strongest level of 2026 and
Ru pee shrugs. extending the rupee's standing as the only sub-continent currency to have appreciated against the
dollar across the entire conflict. The FX market has looked through the 11.7% headline to the 0.5 %
monthly pace and the Brent collapse that will feed the June print, and has concluded the State
Bank's 100 basis point hike to 11.5% has the trajectory contained. With the weekly oil import bill
that Prime Minister Sharif quantified at USD 800 million now deflating alongside Brent, the
external account stress that defined the April shock is unwinding at its source. The currency's
verdict on the inflation print was to ignore it.
Local steel plate prices at PKR 195,000 per ton held, with the USD equivalent near USD 700 per ton
Clock starts. on the firm rupee. Gadani's absolute pricing remains the firmest of the year, the gap to India is
near USD 295 per LDT, and the arrival of the Ron at 21,502 LDT marks the largest unit Gadani has
received since the war opened. The strategic clock, flagged last week, is now visibly running: each
tanker that transits Hormuz under US coordination erodes a little more of the Gulf proximity
premium that anchored Gadani's wartime advantage. A signed deal with a 30-day de-mining
clause would set that

near USD 295 per LDT, and the arrival of the Ron at 21,502 LDT marks the largest unit Gadani has
received since the war opened. The strategic clock, flagged last week, is now visibly running: each
tanker that transits Hormuz under US coordination erodes a little more of the Gulf proximity

TURKEY
I
NFLATION CREEPS, LIRA WAITS
T urkey's May CPI printed at 32.61% year-on-year, up from 32.37% in April and slightly above
e xpectations of 32.5%, the highest reading since October 2025. As in Pakistan, the monthly
Inflation creeps.
c omposition softened materially: consumer prices rose 1.71% on the month, down sharply from
April's 4.18% and the weakest monthly increase since December. Food and non-alcoholic beverages
rose to 34.86% annually, while housing eased slightly to 45.59% and transport to 34.29%. The Istanbul
Chamber's parallel series for the city printed 36.8%. The annual rate creeps higher on arithmetic. The
monthly pulse, for the first time since the war opened, is fading.
The Central Bank of Turkey has suspended its year-end inflation forecast range outright, citing "a
F ramework strained.
period of high uncertainty," while maintaining a 24% year-end target and tightening liquidity by
shifting funding to the 40% overnight lending rate. The suspension of the forecast range is the
institution's most candid admission of the year: the controlled-disinflation framework that the war's
energy shock overran in April cannot yet be re-anchored. USD/TRY steadied near 45.85, just off the
45.90 record, as the broad dollar eased. The Brent collapse toward USD 89, if sustained through a
signed deal, is the disin
snippet: gap to India is near USD 295 per LDT, and the arrival of the Ron at 21,502 LDT marks the largest unit Gadani has received since the war opened. The strategic clock, flagged last week, is now visibly r

text:
shifting funding to the 40% overnight lending rate. The suspension of the forecast range is the
institution's most candid admission of the year: the controlled-disinflation framework that the war's
energy shock overran in April cannot yet be re-anchored. USD/TRY steadied near 45.85, just off the
45.90 record, as the broad dollar eased. The Brent collapse toward USD 89, if sustained through a
signed deal, is the disinflation tailwind the TCMB has waited for all year. The bank has stopped
forecasting whether it will arrive. It is waiting, like everyone else, on the pen.
At USD 268 to USD 290 per LDT across vessel types, Aliaga remains structurally uncompetitive for
N iche persists.
mainstream tonnage, with the gaps to Pakistan and Bangladesh unbridgeable and the gap to India
steady on the rupee's consolidation. Turkey's relevance remains confined to the EU-regulated
tonnage segment, where Basel Convention compliance overrides price. The prospective flood of post-
war tonnage, when it comes, will route overwhelmingly to the sub-continent on the price differential,
and Aliaga's share of it will be set by regulation rather than by markets. That was true through the
war. It will remain true through the peace.

MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
Market sentiment in India remains subdued, with recyclers increasingly concerned
that prevailing price levels are not supported by underlying local market
fundamentals.
Despite weak domestic demand and soft steel market conditions, a pronounced
shortage of available recycling candidates continues to underpin pricing across all
vessel segments.
Ship recyclers remain active in acquiring tonnage at elevated levels, largely to sustain
yard operations and retain workforce capacity rather than on the basis of improved
market economics.
The disconnect between local fundamentals and offered prices persists, although
limited vessel supply is expected to prevent any significant correction in the near term.
BANGLADESH
Following several weeks of aggressive bidding activity, Bangladeshi recyclers have
stepped back from the market, resulting in a correction of approximately USD 25-
30/LDT across all vessel categories.
Prior to the national budget announcement, breakers actively secured inventory amid
concerns over the potential introduction of heavier taxation measures.
Budget-related fears have since eased, with the government implementing only a
modest VAT increase from BDT 1,200 to BDT 1,500, a significantly milder outcome than
initially anticipated.
While recyclers with open yard capacity remain interested in fresh acquisitions, buying
appetite is now being expressed at notably lower and more realistic price levels.
PAKISTAN
Market sentiment in Pakistan remains unchanged from the previous week, with no
major movement reported across pricing or demand.
Local recyclers continue to maintain the same stance as last week, with market activity
remaining steady but without any fresh developments.
The coming week may provide clearer direction, depending on fresh tonnage
availability and

PAKISTAN
Market sentiment in Pakistan remains unchanged from the previous week, with no
major movement reported across pricing or demand.
Local recyclers continue to maintain the same stance as last week, with market activity
remaining steady but without any fresh developments.
The coming week may provide clearer direction, depending on fresh tonnage
availability and any new market developments.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
Market sentiment in Türkiye remains soft, with recycling prices continuing to trend
lower amid weakening demand.
The market has moved further away from the stronger levels witnessed earlier in the
year, reflecting a gradual deterioration in buyer confidence.
Recyclers are exercising increased caution and remain reluctant to commit
aggressively to fresh purchases until clearer market direction emerges.

text:
The war is over. The United States and Iran digitally signed an interim peace agreement this week,a US official confirming the memorandum has taken effect, with a formal signing in Switzerland set for Friday and the Strait of Hormuz reopening after more than one hundred days closed. President Trump announced the breakthrough from Air Force One en route to the G7 summit at Evian, authorising the strait to open and the US naval blockade to lift, and telling the world's shipowners, "Ships of the World, start your engines." Iran's Supreme National Security Council confirmed that all military operations, on all fronts, including Lebanon, would cease immediately and permanently. The sub-continent, which spent the entire quarter praying for exactly these ships, must now ask them to keep their engines idling until the monsoon lifts.
Brent crude has collapsed to approximately USD 78 per barrel, its lowest since late February and effectively the level at which the war began, erasing the entire premium that carried it to USD 126.41 on April 30. WTI trades near USD 75. The agreement removes sanctions on Iranian oil exports and clears the way for Saudi Arabia, the UAE and Iraq to restart millions of barrels of halted output. The International Energy Agency, which in Week 18 called the Hormuz closure "the largest supply disruption in history," now warns of a coming glut, projecting global supply to rise eight million barrels per day by 2027 against demand growth of two million. The forecast has travelled further than the oil.
Freight has continued to cool as the war premium drains from the dry bulk market. The Baltic Dry Index eased to 2,653 on June 17 from the June 1 peak of 3,222, with daily Capesize earnings falling to USD 35,162 from the USD 49,511 high of late May as the disrupted long-haul trades that inflated Capesize demand begin to normalise. Notably, Supramax and Handysize both firmed through the week, the geared segments most relevant to recycling candidate flow holding even as the headline index falls. The bunker-cost floor and the Capesize premium, the twin forces that kept older tonnage profitably trading through the war, are now deflating together and in earnest.
The currency response divided precisely along the fault line the conflict drew. The three Hormuz-exposed importers all strengthened or held: the Indian Rupee rallied to a five-week high near 94
snippet: The war is over. The United States and Iran digitally signed an interim peace agreement this week,a US official confirming the memorandum has taken effect, with a formal signing in Switzerland set for

text:
ysize both firmed through the week, the geared segments most relevant to recycling candidate flow holding even as the headline index falls. The bunker-cost floor and the Capesize premium, the twin forces that kept older tonnage profitably trading through the war, are now deflating together and in earnest.
The currency response divided precisely along the fault line the conflict drew. The three Hormuz-exposed importers all strengthened or held: the Indian Rupee rallied to a five-week high near 94.60 as the collapse in oil prices improved its external balances, the Pakistani Rupee held its 2026 best near 278.28, and the Bangladeshi Taka held the floor of its band near 122.70. The Turkish Lira, alone, broke through 46 per dollar to a fresh record near 46.15, its slide driven by domestic structural inflation that the peace does nothing to address. The deal rescued the oil importers. It had nothing to say to Ankara.
And so the arc closes. Brent climbed past USD 126 and has returned to USD 78, roughly where it began; the barrel toured the war and came home. The strait the war closed has reopened, the blockade has lifted, and the premium that kept the world's ageing tonnage profitably at sea is evaporating from the fuel side and the freight side at once, which means the deferred wave of recycling candidates that eleven weeks of war held back is now, finally, being primed to break. It will break onto a sub-continent in the deep monsoon. The ships are free to sail toward a beach where the weather has just closed. Peace is signed. The rains reign. For the first time since February, the only thing standing between the yards and the tonnage is the sky.
For Week 25 of 2026, GMS Market Rankings / Vessel indications are as below.

BANGLADESH

EACE LANDS, RAINS HOLD
Bangladesh enters Week 25 with USD/BDT near the floor of its band at approximately 122.75, the
Band holds.
level at which the Taka has now closed the entire war chapter without a single breach of the 122.74
to 123.18 range. The collapse in Brent toward USD 78, the broad easing of the dollar on the peace
agreement, and the rupee's rally have all reinforced the band rather than tested it. Foreign exchange
reserves near USD 34.12 billion remain ample, and with the global risk premium now draining out
of every market, the central bank's management burden has fallen to its lightest since before
February. Local steel plate prices held the BDT 65,000 level, with the USD equivalent near USD 530
per ton, the mill complex awaiting direction from the post-war scrap and billet markets rather than
setting it.
The end of the war reshapes Bangladesh's second-half setup more favourably than any single

The end of the war reshapes Bangladesh's second-half setup more favourably than any single event
Peace lands.
of the year. The reopening of Hormuz and the collapse of the bunker-cost floor remove the central
reason owners held ageing tonnage at sea through the conflict, which means the candidate pool
that starved Chattogram for eleven weeks is now being primed to release. The interim peace
agreement, the lifting of the US blockade, and the removal of sanctions on Iranian oil collectively
point to a normalising freight and fuel environment through July and August. Chattogram enters
that environment with the basin's strongest demand position intact: full plot capacity, a cleared
Letter of Credit pipeline, and the region's most stable currency.
The obstacle is now, simply, the sky. The Bay of Bengal monsoon governs the beaching calendar
Ra ins hold.
through August, regardless of how favourable the macro setup becomes, and the deferred tonnage
wave, when it sails, will arrive at a coast that cannot beach at full capacity until the rains lift. The
sequencing matters: if the candidates release through July as the war premium evaporates, and the
monsoon eases into September, Chattogram could face the strongest post-monsoon demand-and-
supply alignment in several years. The war that closed the strait could not fill the beach. The peace
that reopened it cannot, for now, fill it either. But the reason has changed from a blockade to a
season, and a season, unlike a war, keeps a schedule.

UPEE RALLIES, MONSOON RULES
India is the single largest currency beneficiary of the peace. USD/INR rallied to a five-week high near
94.60, appreciating sharply from the 96.97 record of May 21 as Brent's collapse toward USD 78 fed
R upee rallies.
directly into the external-account arithmetic of an economy that imports more than 80% of its crude.
The MoU's finalisation over the weekend, the reopening of Hormuz, and the prospect of restored
capital inflows combined to take the conflict premium out of the rupee in a way that seven weeks of
intervention could not. India's May trade deficit had already narrowed slightly to USD 28.21 billion
even as petroleum imports surged to USD 22.7 billion; the June figures, struck against USD 78 Brent
rather than USD 107, will tell a materially better story.
The rupee's rally is the cleanest expression yet of the war's end, but it is not unconditional. Foreign
institutional investors were still net sellers in places through the week as global growth uncertainty I nflows return.
lingered and as reports cautioned that shipping and energy flows may take weeks to fully normalise.
The interim deal leaves the nuclear question and the durability of the Lebanon ceasefire unresolved,
and a five-week-high rupee carries embedded expectations that the implementation must now meet.
The Reserve Bank of India's June 5 inflow measures, the bond capital-gains tax removal among them,
are now operating into a tailwind rather than a headwind. The currency has repriced the war's end. It
now waits on the war's end to be delivered.

PAKISTAN
R
UPEE HOLDS, PREMIUM FADES
Pakistan closes the war chapter as it spent it, as the basin's currency anchor. USD/PKR held near
Ru pee holds. 278.28, sustaining the strongest level of 2026 and finishing the conflict as the only sub-continent
currency to have appreciated against the dollar across its entire span. The State Bank's 100 basis
point hike to 11.5%, disciplined reserve management, and resilient remittance inflows delivered a
rupee that not only survived the most volatile quarter in years but strengthened through it. With
Brent

text:
's currency anchor. USD/PKR held near
Ru pee holds. 278.28, sustaining the strongest level of 2026 and finishing the conflict as the only sub-continent
currency to have appreciated against the dollar across its entire span. The State Bank's 100 basis
point hike to 11.5%, disciplined reserve management, and resilient remittance inflows delivered a
rupee that not only survived the most volatile quarter in years but strengthened through it. With
Brent now near USD 78, the weekly oil import bill that Prime Minister Sharif quantified at USD
800 million at the height of the war is collapsing toward its pre-conflict level, removing the
external-account pressure that drove the April CPI shock at its source.
The peace, however, begins immediately to dismantle the specific advantage that made Gadani the
Pr emium fades. basin's firmest market during the war. The Gulf proximity premium, which compounded
structurally while Hormuz was closed and Gulf-region tonnage reassessed its positioning, now
faces direct erosion as the strait reopens and routing economics normalise. The strategic clock that
the Weekly first flagged three weeks ago is now running at full speed: each tanker that transits a
reopened Hormuz, each barrel of restarted Gulf production, returns the maritime geography to its
pre-war shape, in which Gadani competes on price and compliance like everyone else rather than
on a wartime locational edge. The advantage was real. It was also, as the Weekly noted,
circumstantial. The circumstance has just ended.
Local steel plate prices at PKR 195,000 per ton held, with the USD equivalent near USD 700 per ton
Position transitions. on the firm rupee, Gadani retaining the firmest absolute pricing in the basin for now. Three HKC-
certified yards remain operational with further certifications in the pipeline, and the Ron at 21,502
LDT continues to work through the yard. Pakistan enters the post-war period from the strongest
position any destination held during the conflict, but it enters a different competition: the wartime
premium that lifted Gadani is fading, the deferred tonnage wave will route on price and
compliance once the monsoon lifts, and the basin's competitive order will reset to its structural
fundamentals. Pakistan consolidated the war better than anyone. The peace will measure what that
consolidation is worth at normal tide

TURKEY
L
IRA BREAKS 46, NICHE HOLDS
T urkey enters Week 25 with USD/TRY having broken through 46 per dollar to a fresh record near
4 6.15, having touched 46.44 intraday, the lira down approximately 17.2% over the past 12 months
Fresh record.
a nd roughly 7% year-to-date. The break is the clearest possible demonstration of the conflict's
asymmetry: in the very week the war ended and every Hormuz-exposed currency in the basin
firmed, the lira made a new low. Turkey's pressures are domestic and structural, the product of an
inflation problem that long predates the war and that the peace does nothing to resolve. The Central
Bank of Turkey's managed-depreciation framework continues to function as designed, the slide
orderly, the level steadily worse.
The May CPI at 32.61%, with the year-end forecast range suspended and the official target held at
I nflation governs.
24%, remains the operative read, and the central bank's policy rate stays at 37% against an effective
40% overnight lending rate. The one channel through which the peace genuinely helps Turkey is
energy: a Brent price near USD 78 rather than USD 107 lowers the imported-inflation pulse that drove
the April acceleration, and a sustained post-war oil environment is the most plausible disinflation
tailwind the TCMB has had all year. It arrives, however, into an inflation structure so entrenched
that even the largest favourable oil moves in a decade register as a marginal improvement rather
than a turn.

the sub-continent on the price differential once the monsoon lifts, and Aliaga's share of it will
continue to be set by EU regulation and Basel Convention compliance rather than by price. Turkey
occupied a different market throughout the entire war. It will occupy the same market throughout
the peace. The conflict changed everything in the basin except Aliaga's position, which is precisely
where it began.

[Page 2]
MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
Market sentiment in India has weakened, with local ship recycling prices correcting
across all segments in line with softer domestic fundamentals.
Most recyclers continue to acquire tonnage despite operating at unprofitable levels,
primarily to maintain business continuity and keep yard operations running.
Labour shortages that disrupted yard operations over recent months have largely
been resolved; however, extreme heat conditions continue to significantly slow
production levels.
With production expected to improve once weather conditions normalize, the
resulting increase in steel supply could place additional downward pressure on local
prices amid persistently weak domestic demand.
BANGLADESH
The negative price correction witnessed last week has curtailed aggressive bidding
activity, with recyclers adopting a notably more cautious approach.
Breakers remain bearish in their outlook, anticipating further price declines in the near
term before re-entering the market aggressively.
Weak domestic steel fundamentals, characterized by declining steel prices and
subdued demand, continue to weigh heavily on recycling activity.
As a result, most recyclers have shifted to a "wait-and-watch" approach, preferring to
monitor market direction before committing to fresh purchases.
PAKISTAN
Market activity remained subdued during the week, with only three working days
limiting overall trading activity.
No significant changes were observed in pricing, demand, or recyclers' buying
sentiment compared to the previous week.
The market remains largely unchanged, with participants awaiting a full working week
for any meaningful developments.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
Market sentiment in Türkiye remains soft, primarily due to lower mining raw material
prices, which have placed downward pressure on domestic steel prices.

text:
The war is over; the paperwork is taking longer than the peace. The formal signing of the US-Iranagreement at Bürgenstock in Switzerland was abruptly postponed last Friday, with Vice President Vance turning his plane around over what the White House called unresolved logistical issues, even as the interim memorandum remained in effect and implementation accelerated on the water. Vessel traffic through the Strait of Hormuz doubled in 24 hours to its highest level since late February, with at least 34 ships exiting into the Gulf of Oman against a pre-war daily average of 110. The Vice President noted that Iran had, for a second consecutive night, declined to fire on any vessel. The ships, having no plane to turn around, simply kept sailing.
Brent crude has fallen below USD 74 per barrel, its lowest since before the war began and now only about 7% above its pre-conflict level, having shed more than 20% in June alone. WTI trades below USD 70. The IEA estimates the UAE is already exporting at roughly 85% of pre-war volumes and warns of a coming glut; OPEC's secretary general rejects both the glut and the notion of a demand peak, leaving the two agencies agreed on nothing except that the other is mistaken. Beneath the collapse sits an oddity: US crude inventories have fallen to their lowest since 1984, with Cushing below operational minimums, a tightness the price has chosen to ignore in favour of the reopening.
Freight has continued its orderly descent from the war peak. The Baltic Dry Index eased to 2,634 on June 24 from the June 1 high of 3,222, with daily Capesize earnings settling near USD 35,800 as the disrupted long-haul trades that inflated demand through the conflict normalise. The geared segments most relevant to recycling held firm, with Supramax near USD 21,400 and Handysize near USD 17,000 per day. The Capesize premium that kept the largest ageing tonnage trading has now fully unwound, and the freight side of the equation that held candidates at sea through the war has, like the fuel side, returned toward its pre-war shape.
The currency picture has acquired a second force. The Indian Rupee firmed to a six-week high near 94.30 on the oil collapse and improving capital inflows, the Pakistani Rupee strengthened to a fresh 2026 best near 278
snippet: The war is over; the paperwork is taking longer than the peace. The formal signing of the US-Iranagreement at Bürgenstock in Switzerland was abruptly postponed last Friday, with Vice President Vance t

text:
day. The Capesize premium that kept the largest ageing tonnage trading has now fully unwound, and the freight side of the equation that held candidates at sea through the war has, like the fuel side, returned toward its pre-war shape.
The currency picture has acquired a second force. The Indian Rupee firmed to a six-week high near 94.30 on the oil collapse and improving capital inflows, the Pakistani Rupee strengthened to a fresh 2026 best near 278.10, and the Bangladeshi Taka held the floor of its band near 122.70. But the dollar itself is now strengthening: the Dollar Index has reached its highest in more than a year as US inflation climbed to 4.2%, a three-year high, and markets price roughly a 75% chance of a Federal Reserve rate increase by September. The Turkish Lira, domestic to its core, slid past 46 toward 46.40, a fresh record. The energy shock that drove inflation across the sub-continent has ended; America's inflation, meanwhile, has reached a three-year high. The fire did not go out. It changed address.
This is Ashura week, and the sub-continent's yards are largely closed for the holiday and slowed by the monsoon both. Around them, two queues are forming. One is the growing line of cargoes waiting to move through a reopening Hormuz, which analysts now watch by the day. The other is the deferred wave of recycling candidates that the war's end is finally releasing, with the Andhika Paramesti sold to Bangladesh at USD 460 last week the first of what should become many. Between the tonnage and the beach stands the monsoon, which serves both queues with equal indifference and will not lift before September. The strait fills. The queue builds. The yards, for a few more weeks, wait. The war that would not end has ended. The season that always ends has not.
For Week 26 of 2026, GMS Market Rankings / Vessel indications are as below.

AKA HOLDS, WAVE FORMS
Bangladesh enters Week 26 with USD/BDT near the floor of its band at approximately 122.70, the
Taka holds.
Taka having now held the 122.74 to 123.18 range intact through the war and into the peace without
a single breach. The collapse in Brent below USD 74, down more than 20% on the month, removes
the imported-energy pressure that was the band's principal external risk, and the broad
strengthening of the dollar on US rate expectations has been absorbed without strain. Foreign
exchange reserves near USD 34.12 billion remain the operative buffer. Local steel plate prices
moved from BDT 65,000 to BDT 67,000 through the week, with

text:
a single breach. The collapse in Brent below USD 74, down more than 20% on the month, removes
the imported-energy pressure that was the band's principal external risk, and the broad
strengthening of the dollar on US rate expectations has been absorbed without strain. Foreign
exchange reserves near USD 34.12 billion remain the operative buffer. Local steel plate prices
moved from BDT 65,000 to BDT 67,000 through the week, with the USD equivalent rising from
approximately USD 535 to USD 551 per ton, the mill complex still taking direction from a post-war
scrap market that is searching for its floor rather than setting one.
The first concrete evidence of the deferred tonnage wave has arrived. The Andhika Paramesti, a
Wave forms.
9,369 LDT bulker, was sold to Bangladesh at USD 460 per LDT on an as-is Sambu basis, the first
reported sale into the basin since the strait reopened and a marker that the candidate pool the war
held back is beginning to release. It is one vessel, not a flood, and it lands in a week shortened by
Ashura and constrained by the monsoon, but the direction is established: with the bunker-cost floor
and the Capesize premium both deflating, owners who held ageing tonnage at sea through the
conflict now have materially less reason to keep holding it. Chattogram is positioned to absorb the
wave when it builds, the question being one of timing rather than appetite.
Ashura, observed June 26, has brought Chattogram's cash-buyer activity to its customary holiday
Ho liday slows.
pause, layered atop the monsoon's seasonal reduction in beaching cycles. The Letter of Credit
pipeline remains in its most permissive posture of the year, full plot capacity is available, and the
currency is the most stable in the basin, which means the only binding constraint on Bangladesh
converting the incoming wave into beached tonnage is the calendar. The structural setup for the
third quarter is the strongest the market has carried since before February: strong demand, cleared
financing, a stable Taka, a reopening supply pipeline, and a monsoon that, unlike the war, has a
known end date. The wave forms offshore. The beach waits for the sky.

through 94 is being met by a dollar strengthening on America's own inflation problem. The rupee is
winning the argument it can control and being held back by the one it cannot.

weekly oil import bill, which Prime Minister Sharif quantified at USD 800 million at the war's
height, back toward its pre-conflict level, relieving the external-account pressure that produced
April's double-digit CPI shock at its source. The State Bank's rate posture and disciplined reserve
management carried the rupee through the conflict and are now compounding with the oil relief.
The specific advantage that made Gadani the basin's firmest market through the war has now
Pr emium gone. substantially gone. The Gulf proximity premium, which compounded while Hormuz was closed
and Gulf-region tonnage reassessed its routing, has eroded in real time as the strait reopens: with
vessel traffic through Hormuz doubling in a day and the UAE exporting at 85% of pre-war
volumes, the maritime geography that handed Gadani a wartime locational edge has returned to
its ordinary shape. The strategic clock the Weekly began flagging a month ago has, in effect, run
out. Gadani now competes where it always competes in peacetime, on price and on compliance,
and the wartime premium that lifted its position is no longer a factor in the basin's pricing.
Local steel plate prices at PKR 195,000 per ton held, with the USD equivalent near USD 701 per ton
Position resets. on the firm rupee, Gadani retaining the firmest absolute pricing in the basin for the moment. Three
HKC-certified yards remain operational with further certifications in the pipeline, and the Ron at
21,502 LDT has worked through the yard. Pakistan enters the post-war competition from the
strongest position any destination held during the conflict, but it enters a normalised market: the
deferred tonnage wave will route on price and compliance once the monsoon lifts, the wartime
premium is spent, and Gadani's share of the coming supply will be determined by fundamentals
rather than by geography. Pakistan won the war quarter. The peace quarter will be a different
contest, played on different ground.

[Page 2]
MARKET SUMMARY
INDIA
Market sentiment softened further through the week, with local ship recycling prices
continuing to trend downward amid persistent weakness in buying interest.
Discussions remain ongoing between Indian recycling yards and the European Union
regarding the inclusion of three Indian yards on the EU-approved recycling list, with
one of our yards among the applicants.
A noticeable influx of vessels from the dark fleet continues to arrive at Indian recycling
destinations, contributing to market attention despite otherwise subdued trading
conditions.
Overall, the market closed the week on a softer note, with limited fresh developments
outside the ongoing regulatory discussions.
BANGLADESH
The local market remained weak overall, with a brief improvement during the week
proving unsustainable as prices softened again toward the close.
Despite the broader market weakness, buyers have shown exceptionally strong
interest in small- and mid-sized tankers, with few transactions concluded at notably
aggressive price levels.
Demand continues to be concentrated on these smaller tanker units, while buying
appetite for other vessel types remains limited.
Overall market sentiment remains cautious, with recyclers closely monitoring price
direction before expanding their purchasing activity.
PAKISTAN
Market sentiment remained stable during the week, with vessel prices largely
unchanged from the previous week.
A few vessels arrived during the week, providing some activity in an otherwise steady
market.
Recyclers remain keen to acquire fresh tonnage and continue to show interest in
available recycling candidates.
Overall, the market remains balanced, supported by stable pricing and continued
buying interest.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Aliaga market has steadied following the recent correction in prices, though

text:
stable during the week, with vessel prices largely
unchanged from the previous week.
A few vessels arrived during the week, providing some activity in an otherwise steady
market.
Recyclers remain keen to acquire fresh tonnage and continue to show interest in
available recycling candidates.
Overall, the market remains balanced, supported by stable pricing and continued
buying interest.

[Page 3]
TURKIYE
The Aliaga market has steadied following the recent correction in prices, though
overall activity remains limited.
Most yards continue to operate with healthy inventories, reducing their appetite for
fresh tonnage, especially larger and higher-grade units.
The wide price gap with the Indian subcontinent continues to restrict Türkiye's
competitiveness for standard recycling candidates.
As a result, demand remains largely driven by regulatory considerations, with EU-
flagged and EU-compliant vessels still favoring Türkiye despite lower recycling returns.

text:
The war is over, and the fleet is finally moving. Crude flows through Hormuz exceeded 10 million barrels per daythis week under US military coordination, while Lloyd's List counts roughly 550 merchant vessels preparing to exit the Gulf, including some 200 bulk carriers. The central channel remains constrained by an estimated 80 mines, keeping traffic to the northern and southern routes for now, but the direction of travel is no longer theoretical. Tehran continues to decline face-to-face talks with the Americans in Doha. Its cargoes carry no such reservations.
Brent has settled near USD 71 per barrel, effectively back at its pre-war level, closing the quarter with its sharpest fall since 2020 after touching USD 126.41 on April 30. Analysts have cut their 2026 forecasts for the first time since the war began, after five consecutive months of raising them, even as US crude inventories have now drawn for twelve straight weeks. The barrel has completed the round trip. The inventory shortage remains. The price has chosen the reopening.
Freight has adjusted, but not collapsed. The Baltic Dry Index bounced late to approximately 2,650 on iron ore strength, with Capesize earnings near USD 32,060 per day. The index gained 25% for the quarter and lost 22% for the month, which is the cleanest measure yet of the conflict's afterlife in freight. The war's freight premium outlived the war by exactly one month. It is now fading into a market where the ships that stayed trading through the conflict have fewer reasons to keep doing so.
The dollar has taken the war's place as the basin's moving force. The Indian Rupee gave back part of its recovery to near 95.1, the Bangladeshi Taka eased through the top of its long-held band toward 123.2, and the Turkish Lira set another record low near 46.70. The Pakistani Rupee alone strengthened, breaking below 278, while Pakistan's June CPI turned lower to 11.1%, with prices falling outright on the month. Islamabad's July 1 tax and duty SROs have also quietly shifted the ground beneath Gadani, introducing a local-policy variable just as the external war premium disappears.
The yards now face two queues. One is the visible line of vessels exiting the Gulf. The other is the deferred recycling wave that the war held offshore and the monsoon is still holding
snippet: The war is over, and the fleet is finally moving. Crude flows through Hormuz exceeded 10 million barrels per daythis week under US military coordination, while Lloyd's List counts roughly 550 merchant

text:
alone strengthened, breaking below 278, while Pakistan's June CPI turned lower to 11.1%, with prices falling outright on the month. Islamabad's July 1 tax and duty SROs have also quietly shifted the ground beneath Gadani, introducing a local-policy variable just as the external war premium disappears.
The yards now face two queues. One is the visible line of vessels exiting the Gulf. The other is the deferred recycling wave that the war held offshore and the monsoon is still holding from the beaches. The exit queue and the deflating premium govern what will come. The rains and the tide calendar govern when it can land. The ships are moving. The beaches are waiting. The war deferred the wave; the weather now decides its arrival.
For Week 27 of 2026, GMS Market Rankings / Vessel indications are as below.

while Pakistan's June CPI turned lower to 11.1%, with prices falling outright on the month. Islamabad's July 1
tax and duty SROs have also quietly shifted the ground beneath Gadani, introducing a local-policy variable
just as the external war premium disappears.
The yards now face two queues. One is the visible line of vessels exiting the Gulf. The other is the deferred
recycling wave that the war held offshore and the monsoon is still holding from the beaches. The exit queue

BANGLADESH
B
AND BREAKS, WAVE NEARS
The band that survived one hundred days of war broke up in the third week of peace, to the dollar.
Band breaks.
USD/BDT eased off the 122.70 floor where it spent the conflict and moved through the upper bound
of its long-held 122.74 to 123.18 range, trading toward approximately 123.2 to 123.3 this week, the
first breach of the band in either direction since the war opened. The driver is not Chattogram and
not Hormuz: it is the broad dollar, with the Dollar Index at its highest in more than a year, US
inflation at 4.2%, and markets pricing roughly a 75% chance of a Federal Reserve rate increase by
September. The pressure the Taka absorbed from a war next door is now being taken from a central
bank an ocean away. Foreign exchange reserves near USD 34.12 billion remain the operative buffer,
and the move remains orderly, measured in tenths rather than whole figures. Local steel plate
prices eased fr om th e firmer BDT 67,000 level reached late last week to BDT 66,000 by July 3, with
the USD equivalent holding near USD 543 per ton on the softer Taka.
The deferred tonnage wave now has a census. Lloyd's List estimates roughly 550 merchant vessels
Wave nears.
are preparing to exit the Gulf as the strait normalises, including approximately 200 bulk carriers,
160 tankers, 60 container ships and 10 vehicle carriers, and every unit of ageing tonnage in that
queue exits into a freight market whose Capesize premium has unwound and a bunker market
priced off USD 71 Brent rather than USD 126. Chattogram's anchorage and delivery list are already
showing the early arrivals converting, with the Zamrud and ERGY gas carriers at 28,858 and 31,341
LDT among the largest units delivered during the window, and the Andhika Paramesti sale at USD
460 per LDT two weeks ago still the wave's first executed marker. The Letter of Credit pipeline
remains

priced off USD 71 Brent rather than USD 126. Chattogram's anchorage and delivery list are already
showing the early arrivals converting, with the Zamrud and ERGY gas carriers at 28,858 and 31,341
LDT among the largest units delivered during the window, and the Andhika Paramesti sale at USD
460 per LDT two weeks ago still the wave's first executed marker. The Letter of Credit pipeline
remains fully permissive, plot capacity is available, and the June 30 to July 3 tide window is running
as this issue publishes, with the next window July 14 to 17. The wave is no longer a thesis. It is a
list.
Bangladesh's June CPI from BBS is the operative watch item in the coming days; the April print of
Set up holds.
9.04% remains the reference, and the Pakistan June read, which turned lower on the oil collapse,
suggests the Taka basket's energy pass-through should begin unwinding on the same mechanics.
The structural setup for the second half is unchanged and remains the basin's strongest: full
demand, cleared financing, pricing that holds Chattogram at the top of the rankings, and a
monsoon that lifts into September just as the counted queue converts into candidates. The band's
break at the top costs the mills a few dollars per ton on the import side and hands the breakers a
marginally better Taka realisation per dollar of steel. The season still governs the next eight weeks.
The list now governs everything after.

not, has resolved in the dollar's favour: the Dollar Index at a more-than-one-year high, US inflation at
4.2%, and roughly 75% odds of a September Federal Reserve hike have overpowered the oil tailwind
that should, in isolation, have carried the rupee through 94. The war's pressure on the rupee came
through the import bill. The peace's pressure comes through the Fed. The bill was easier to model.
The transmission now runs almost entirely through the capital account. Foreign institutional flows
remained mixed through the week, with debt inflows supported by the Reserve Bank of India's bond Dollar rules.
capital-gains tax removal but equity flows capped by the global rate repricing, and the rupee's June
range of roughly 94.3 to 95.3 maps almost precisely onto the dollar's own moves rather than anything
domestic. India's June CPI, due mid-month, will be struck against pre-war oil for the first time since
February and should extend the 3.48% April calm; the June trade figures, priced off USD 71 Brent rather
than USD 107, should materially narrow the petroleum bill. The fundamentals are turning supportive
on schedule. The currency is waiting on Washington's inflation problem, not Delhi's.

PAKISTAN
D
UTIES SHIFT, CPI TURNS
The Federal Budget, which at announcement appeared to leave the metal sector untouched, has
Du ties shift. clarified itself. With the issuance and implementation of the SROs from July 1, the actual duty
changes are now visible: Regulatory Duty on re-rollable and melting scrap has been reduced by
1%, worth approximately PKR 1,000 to 1,500 per metric ton, or roughly USD 3.5 to USD 5.5 at
prevailing rates. The larger moves sit in prime products. RD on HR prime imports has been cut by
2.5%, worth approximately PKR 3,500 per ton, or USD 12.5, while the reduction on wire rod
imports delivers a saving near PKR 6,000 per ton, or approximately USD 21.50. The ship recycling
sector itself received no direct changes. The budget, at announcement, appeared to leave the metals
market alone. The SROs, at implementation, confirmed that it had not.
The transmission to Gadani runs through the product rather than the plot. Cheaper imported scrap
Co mpetition firms. applies moderate pressure, with buyers positioned to press for relatively cheaper local material,
but the more consequential moves are HR prime and wire rod: at USD 12.5 and USD 21.50 per ton
of new duty relief, imported prime product becomes materially more competitive against locally
recycled and re-rolled material, sharpening the ceiling over what Gadani's output can command.
The sector that received no direct changes in the budget is the one now expecting the pressure. The
effect is assessed as moderate rather than significant on the scrap side and more noticeable through
the prime channel, and it arrives in the first fiscal week after the wartime Gulf premium finished
eroding, making it the opening structural test of Gadani's post-war pricing.
Pakistan's June CPI eased to 11.1% year-on-year from May's 11.7%, the first annual deceleration
CPI turns. since the war opened, and consumer prices fell outright by 0.3% on the month,

Pakistan's June CPI eased to 11.1% year-on-year from May's 11.7%, the first annual deceleration
CPI turns. since the war opened, and consumer prices fell outright by 0.3% on the month, the first monthly
decline of the conflict era, as the Brent collapse fed directly into the domestic fuel complex. Prices
in Pakistan fell in the month; the smoke alarm has finally heard about the fire. The Wholesale Price
Index slowed to 10.7%, with a 1.2% monthly decline, urban core eased to 8.7%, and the full fiscal
year closed at an average of 7.05%, under the government's 7.5% target despite petrol printing a
record PKR 458.40 per litre in April. USD/PKR broke below 278 to trade near 277.7 to 278.0, a fresh
2026 best, stronger by roughly 2.1% over twelve months and still the basin's only appreciating
currency. Local steel plate held at PKR 195,000 per ton, near USD 696 on the firm rupee, with the
Jenny Lucky and Ladonna bulkers the newest arrivals at Gadani. The macro turn is in. The duty
structure beneath the plate quote has just moved, and July will measure which force the breakers
feel first.

37% for a third consecutive meeting in June, encouraged by the May monthly inflation print easing
to 1.71%, while the effective 40% overnight funding rate continues to tighten. The framework
functions. The level, as it has all year, worsens on schedule.
Turkey's June CPI prints July 3, the day this issue publishes, and it is the first reading struck
Print looms.
substantially against post-war oil. The May print of 32.61% annual and 1.71% monthly remains the
operative read, the year-end forecast range remains suspended with the 24% target nominally intact,
and independent estimates continue to track materially above the official series. Brent near USD 71,
against USD 107 in mid-May and the USD 126 peak, is the genuine disinflation tailwind the TCMB
has waited for all year, and the June and July prints will show whether it can bend an inflation
structure still measured in the thirties. The bank stopped forecasting in May. The data resumes
speaking tomorrow.

carriers preparing to leave the Gulf will route overwhelmingly to the sub-continent on the price
differential once the monsoon lifts, while Aliaga's share of the wave will continue to be set by EU
regulation and Basel Convention compliance rather than by price. The lira's slide toward 47 firms
Turkish offers marginally in dollar terms without bridging a gap measured in hundreds. Turkey
occupied a separate market through the war, occupies it through the peace, and will occupy it
through the wave.
